critique.summary: The article critiques the modern web experience of major news websites, focusing on the excessive data usage, intrusive ads, and hostile user interface patterns that degrade user experience. It highlights the New York Times as an example of a 49MB page load with numerous tracking scripts and programmatic ad auctions that harm performance and privacy. The author argues that these practices prioritize short-term ad revenue over long-term reader retention and user respect.
mainPoints: The New York Times homepage loads 49MB of data with 422 network requests, causing slow performance and high resource usage.
Modern news sites heavily rely on programmatic ad auctions running in the browser, which tax CPU and degrade user experience.
There is extensive user tracking and behavioral surveillance embedded in news websites, often hidden behind consent banners.
Hostile UX patterns such as intrusive modals, multiple pop-ups, and sticky video players increase interaction cost and frustrate readers.
Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) caused by late-loading ads disrupts reading flow and is penalized by Google SEO guidelines.
Publishers prioritize metrics like time-on-page and viewability, leading to adversarial design choices that harm user trust.
Simplified, lightweight news versions like text.npr.org exist and offer better user experience without bloat or tracking.
The current architecture results from systemic incentive problems rather than individual decisions by engineers.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims about the New York Times page size and number of network requests are consistent with independent web performance analyses available publicly, which show large page sizes and numerous third-party scripts on major news sites. The description of programmatic ad auctions and tracking beacons aligns with known ad tech practices documented by privacy researchers and web performance experts. The critique of hostile UX patterns such as intrusive modals, sticky videos, and CLS is supported by established usability heuristics and Google's Core Web Vitals metrics. However, the article is largely opinionated and anecdotal, relying on a single example and personal observations without citing multiple independent sources or presenting counterarguments. The economic analysis of publisher incentives is plausible but not substantiated with direct evidence or industry data. Overall, the factual elements are accurate but the article lacks journalistic rigor and balanced perspectives.
considerations: The article does not provide data from multiple news websites to generalize the problem beyond the New York Times and a few examples.
It does not include perspectives from publishers, advertisers, or ad tech companies to explain the rationale behind these design choices.
There is no discussion of recent efforts or technologies aimed at improving web performance or privacy on news sites.
The article overlooks the potential benefits publishers gain from ad revenue that supports free journalism.
It does not address user agency in choosing ad blockers or alternative news consumption methods.
The publication date is in the future (March 12th, 2026), which raises questions about the article's authenticity or timing.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the excessive bloat and resource usage of modern websites, especially news sites, with many users expressing frustration over ads, heavy JavaScript, and privacy concerns. Some comments reflect on the historical context of web development and journalism economics, while others share personal strategies to mitigate these issues.

comments.author: hilbert42
comments.text:
These days the NYT is in a race to the bottom. I no longer even bother to bypass ads let alone read the news stories because of its page bloat and other annoyances. It's just not worth the effort.

Surely news outlets like the NYT must realize that savvy web surfers like yours truly when encountering "difficult" news sites—those behind firewalls and or with megabytes of JavaScript bloat—will just go elsewhere or load pages without JavaScript.

We'll simply cut the headlines from the offending website and past it into a search engine and find another site with the same or similar info but with easier access.

I no longer think about it as by now my actions are automatic. Rarely do I find an important story that's just limited to only one website, generally dozens have the story and because of syndication the alternative site one selects even has identical text and images.

My default browsing is with JavaScript defaulted to "off" and it's rare that I have to enable it (which I can do with just one click).

I never see Ads on my Android phone or PC and that includes YouTube. Disabling JavaScript on webpages nukes just about all ads, they just vanish, any that escape through are then trapped by other means. In ahort, ads are optional. (YouTube doesn't work sans JS, so just use NewPipe or PipePipe to bypass ads.)

Disabling JavaScript also makes pages blindingly fast as all that unnecessary crap isn't loaded. Also, sans JS it's much harder for websites to violate one's privacy and sell one's data.

Do I feel guilty about skimming off info in this manner? No, not the slightest bit. If these sites played fair then it'd be a different matter but they don't. As they act like sleazebags they deserve to be treated as such.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, realistic user perspective on how heavy ads and JavaScript bloat degrade user experience and privacy, backed by practical strategies.

comments.author: cjs_ac
comments.text:
My family's first broadband internet connection, circa 2005, came with a monthly data quota of 400 MB.

The fundamental problem of journalism is that the economics no longer works out. Historically, the price of a copy of a newspaper barely covered the cost of printing; the rest of the cost was covered by advertising. And there was an awful lot of advertising: everything was advertised in newspapers. Facebook Marketplace and Craigslist were a section of the newspaper, as was whichever website you check for used cars or real estate listings. Journalism had to be subsidised by advertising, because most people aren't actually that interested in the news to pay the full cost of quality reporting; nowadays, the only newspapers that are thriving are those that aggressively target those who have an immediate financial interest in knowing what's going on: the Financial Times, Bloomberg, and so on.

The fact is that for most people, the news was interesting because it was new every day. Now that there is a more compelling flood of entertainment in television and the internet, news reporting is becoming a niche product.

The lengths that news websites are going to to extract data from their readers to sell to data brokers is just a last-ditch attempt to remain profitable.
comments.analysis: This comment offers an insightful economic explanation for the rise of intrusive ads and data collection on news sites, aligning well with known industry trends.

comments.author: snickerer
comments.text:
Allowing scripting on websites (in the mid-90s) was a completely wrong decision. And an outrage. Programs are downloaded to my computer and executed without me being able to review them first—or rely on audits by people I trust. That’s completely unacceptable; it’s fundamentally flawed. Of course, you disable scripts on websites. But there are sites that are so broken that they no longer work properly, since the developers are apparently so confused that they assume people only view their pages with JavaScript enabled.

It would have been so much better if we had simply decided back in the ’90s that executable programs and HTML don’t belong together. The world would be so much better today.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a valid critique of client-side scripting's security and privacy implications, though it overlooks the benefits and evolution of web interactivity.

comments.author: PunchyHamster
comments.text:
Our developers managed to run around 750MB per website open once.

They have put in ticket with ops that the server is slow and could we look at it. So we looked. Every single video on a page with long video list pre-loaded a part of it. The single reason the site didn't ran like shit for them is coz office had direct fiber to out datacenter few blocks away.

We really shouldn't allow web developers more than 128kbit of connection speed, anything more and they just make nonsense out of it.
comments.analysis: The anecdote about extreme data usage is plausible and highlights real-world issues, but the suggestion to limit developers' connection speeds is an emotional overreaction.

comments.author: vsgherzi
comments.text: Modern web dev is ridiculous. Most websites are an ad ridden tracking hellacape. Seeing sites like hn where lines of js are taken seriously is a godsend. Make the web less bloated.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a common frustration with web bloat and tracking, though it is somewhat hyperbolic and lacks nuance.

comments.author: zahlman
comments.text:
This site more or less practices what it preaches. `newsbanner.webp` is 87.1KB (downloaded and saved; the Network tab in Firefox may report a few times that and I don't know why); the total image size is less than a meg and then there's just 65.6KB of HTML and 15.5 of CSS.

And it works without JavaScript... but there does appear to be some tracking stuff. A deferred call out to Cloudflare, a hit counter I think? and some inline stuff at the bottom that defers some local CDN thing the old-fashioned way. Noscript catches all of this and I didn't feel like allowing it in order to weigh it.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a concrete example supporting the article's claims, showing how a minimal site can function well without heavy scripts.

comments.author: dizzy9
comments.text:
I remember in 2008, when Wizards of the Coast re-launched the official Dungeons & Dragons website to coincide with the announcement of the fourth edition rules. The site was something in the region of 4 MB, plus a 20 MB embedded video file. A huge number of people were refreshing the site to see what the announcement was, and it was completely slammed. Nobody could watch the trailer until they uploaded it to YouTube later.

4 MB was an absurd size for a website in 2008. It's still an absurd size for a website.
comments.analysis: This historical example illustrates how web bloat has been an issue for many years, providing useful context.

comments.author: userbinator
comments.text:
I also use and like the comparison in units of Windows 95 installs (~40MB), which is also rather ironic in that Win95 was widely considered bloated when it was released.

While this article focuses on ads, it's worth noting that sites have had ads for a long time, but it's their obnoxiousness and resource usage that's increased wildly over time. I wouldn't mind small sponsored links and (non-animated!) banners, but the moment I enable JS to read an article and it results in a flurry of shit flying all over the page and trying to get my attention, I leave promptly.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a relevant analogy and highlights the evolution of ads from simple to resource-heavy, supporting the discussion well.

comments.author: galphanet
comments.text:
This is just the top of the iceberg. Don't get me started on airlines websites (looking at you Air Canada), where the product owner, designers, developers are not able to get a simple workflow straight without loading Mb of useless javascript and interrupt the user journey multiple times. Give me back the command line terminal like Amadeus, that would be perfect.

How can we go back to a Web where websites are designed to be used by the user and not for the shareholders?
comments.analysis: While emotional, this comment reflects common user frustrations with poorly designed, bloated websites, especially in specific industries.

comments.author: the_snooze
comments.text: It's really hard to consider any kind of web dev as "engineering." Outcomes like this show that they don't have any particular care for constraints. It's throw-spaghetti-at-the-wall YOLO programming.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a harsh but partially justified critique of modern web development practices lacking discipline.

comments.author: h4ch1
comments.text:
This rubbish also exists disproportionately for recipe pages/cooking websites as well.

You have 20 ads scattered around, an autoplaying video of some random recipe/ad, 2-3 popups to subscribe, buy some affiliated product and then the author's life story and then a story ABOUT the recipe before I am able to see the detailed recipe in the proper format.

It's second nature to open all these websites in reader mode for me atp.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a specific example of bloat and intrusive ads, supporting the broader critique though somewhat anecdotal.

comments.author: shevy-java
comments.text: Ublock origin helps mitigate at the least a little bit here.
comments.analysis: A brief but accurate suggestion for mitigating ad bloat, though it lacks depth.

comments.author: decimalenough
comments.text: This is why people continue to lament Google Reader (and RSS in general): it was a way to read content on your own terms, without getting hijacked by ads.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly highlights the value of RSS for ad-free content consumption, relevant to the discussion.

comments.author: napolux
comments.text: and the NYT web team was praised as one of the best in the world some (many?) years ago.
comments.analysis: A factual statement providing context about NYT's past reputation, though not deeply analytical.

comments.author: mrb
comments.text:
Let's play a fun prediction: I ask HN readers what will be the page size of NYTimes.com in 10 years? Or 20 years?

Want to bet 100 MB? 1 GB? Is it unthinkable?

20 years ago, a 49 MB home page was unthinkable.
comments.analysis: A speculative but reasonable comment about future trends in page size growth, adding perspective.

comments.author: mvrckhckr
comments.text: Only major media can get away with this kind of bloat. For the normal website, Google would never include you in the SERPs even if your page is a fraction of that size.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly notes SEO implications and how large bloat is tolerated mainly by major media sites.

comments.author: Crowberry
comments.text:
I hate this trend of active distraction. Most blogs have a popup asking you to subscribe as soon as you start scrolling.

It’s as if everyone designed their website around the KPI of irritating your visitors and getting them to leave ASAP.
comments.analysis: A common user complaint about intrusive subscription popups, reflecting genuine annoyance but not analytical depth.

comments.author: throwatdem12311
comments.text: 49mb web page?  Try a 45mb graphql response.
comments.analysis: A brief comment pointing out another form of data bloat, but lacks elaboration.

comments.author: Bratmon
comments.text: Maybe I'm just getting old, but I've gotten tired of these "Journalists shouldn't try to make their living by finding profitable ads, they should just put in ads that look pretty but pay almost nothing and supplement their income by working at McDonalds" takes.
comments.analysis: An emotional comment dismissing critiques of journalism's economic models without substantive argument.

comments.author: frereubu
comments.text: When working at the BBC in the late 90s, the ops team would start growling at you if a site's home page was over 70kb...
comments.analysis: A nostalgic anecdote illustrating how much web page sizes have grown, accurate but limited in insight.

comments.author: lousken
comments.text:
rule #1 is to always give your js devs only core 2 quad cpus + 16GB of RAM

they won't be able to complain about low memory but their experience will be terrible every time they try to shove something horrible into the codebase
comments.analysis: A sarcastic comment that is more humorous than constructive or accurate.

comments.author: ray023
comments.text:
I think it's a GOOD thing, actually. Because all these publications a dying anyway. And even if your filter out all the ad and surveillance trash, you are left with trash propaganda and brain rot content. Like why even make the effort of filtering out the actual text from some "journalist" from these propaganda outlets. It's not even worth it.

If people tune out only because how horrible the sites are, good.
comments.analysis: An emotional and somewhat cynical viewpoint that dismisses the value of news sites, reflecting personal bias.
